chunk_summary: Guidelines for classifying acute malnutrition and determining outpatient versus inpatient care decisions based on severity, complications, and community-linked management strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the classification criteria for severe and moderate acute malnutrition (SAM and MAM).
Learn to differentiate uncomplicated versus complicated malnutrition for site-of-care decisions.
Explore community-linked outpatient management strategies for malnutrition in Indian practice.
key_facts: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is classified as WLZ/WHZ < -3 SD, MUAC <115 mm, or bilateral pitting edema for children aged 6–59 months.
Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is classified as WLZ/WHZ between -3 and < -2 SD or MUAC 115–124 mm without bilateral edema.
Infants under 6 months require weight-for-length, visible wasting, edema, and feeding risk assessments instead of MUAC thresholds.
Uncomplicated malnutrition is managed outpatient with structured nutrition rehabilitation and frequent monitoring.
Complicated malnutrition requires inpatient stabilization for conditions like poor appetite, severe edema, hypothermia, or altered sensorium.
reference_quote: For children aged 6–59 months, classify severe acute malnutrition (SAM) as WLZ/WHZ < -3 SD and/or MUAC <115 mm and/or bilateral pitting edema. (WHO Child Growth Standards)
